{{Coord|34|28|29.47|N|135|46|42.24|E|region:JP-29|display=title|name=桝山古墳（倭彦命 身狭桃花鳥坂墓）}} {{デフォルトソート:ますやまこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

#転送 [[倭彦命#墓]]
{{Coord|34|28|29.47|N|135|46|42.24|E|region:JP-29|display=title|name=桝山古墳（伝倭彦命墓）}} {{デフォルトソート:ますやまこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

revision.comment: 新しいページ（リダイレクト起こし）：桝山古墳（奈良県橿原市にある古墳）。
revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 桝山古墳 |画像 = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
左に墳丘、右に付加前方部 |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 29.47 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 46|経度秒 = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺85m
高さ15m |築造年代 = [[5世紀]]前半 |埋葬施設 = 不明 |被葬者 = （[[宮内庁]]治定）[[倭彦命]] |出土品 = [[埴輪]]片 |史跡指定 = 宮内庁治定「身狭桃花鳥坂墓」 |特記事項 = 方墳としては全国第1位の規模 |地図 = Japan Nara |ラベル = 桝山古墳 |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[倭彦命]]身狭桃花鳥坂墓 拝所}}]] '''桝山古墳'''（ますやまこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある古墳。形状は[[方墳]]。 実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]により「'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（むさのつきさかのはか）」として第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている。 方墳としては全国で最大規模の古墳である。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、貝吹山北麓の尾根先端部に築造された古墳である{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。現在は[[宮内庁]]治定の皇族墓として同庁の管理下にある。 墳形は元々は方形であるが、後世に北東部に前方部が付加されて前方後円形に生垣が巡らされている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘は一辺約85メートル{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}（または一辺約90メートル[http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）]（橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」）。）・高さ約15メートルを測るが、これは方墳としては全国で最大規模になる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘は3段築成{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘表面では[[埴輪]]片が検出されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。その他については詳らかでない。 この桝山古墳は、[[古墳時代]]中期の[[5世紀]]前半頃（または5世紀末-[[6世紀]]前半頃{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}）の築造と推定される。被葬者は明らかでないが、前述のように現在は宮内庁により[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。本古墳の北方には[[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]]（[[宣化天皇]]身狹桃花鳥坂上陵）が、西方には[[新沢千塚古墳群]]があり、それらとの関係性が指摘される{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。 == 被葬者 == 桝山古墳の実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]では第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の'''[[倭彦命]]'''（やまとひこのみこと）の墓に治定している宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}『陵墓地形図集成 縮小版』 宮内庁書陵部陵墓課編、学生社、2014年、p. 400。。倭彦命について、『日本書紀』では垂仁天皇28年に薨去して「身狭桃花鳥坂（むさのつきさか）」に葬られたとし、その際の殉死の悲惨な様によって殉死の禁令が出されたとする（埴輪起源説話の発端）{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠く{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。 その後、倭彦命墓の所在に関する所伝は失われ、[[江戸時代]]には『大和志』・『大和名所図会』等において[[鬼の爼・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）4月に[[内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、[[1886年]]（明治19年）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、[[1890年]]（明治23年）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。ただしその治定には否定的な見解が強い{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=石井茂輔|year=|chapter=身狭桃花鳥坂墓|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 30 奈良県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=[[大塚初重]]|year=1989|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）] - 橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」 {{デフォルトソート:ますやまこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 30 奈良県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=[[大塚初重]]|year=1989|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[倭彦命]] * [[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）] - 橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」 {{デフォルトソート:ますやまこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 桝山古墳 |画像 = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
左に墳丘、右に付加前方部 |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 29.47 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 46|経度秒 = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺85m
高さ15m |築造年代 = [[5世紀]]前半 |埋葬施設 = 不明 |被葬者 = （[[宮内庁]]治定）[[倭彦命]] |出土品 = [[埴輪]]片 |史跡指定 = 宮内庁治定「身狭桃花鳥坂墓」 |特記事項 = 方墳としては全国第1位の規模 |地図 = Japan Nara |ラベル = 桝山古墳 |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[倭彦命]]身狭桃花鳥坂墓 拝所}}]] '''桝山古墳'''（ますやまこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある古墳。形状は[[方墳]]。 実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]により「'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（むさのつきさかのはか、身狹桃花鳥坂墓）」として第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている。 方墳としては全国で最大規模の古墳である。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、貝吹山北麓の尾根先端部に築造された古墳である{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。「桝山（ますやま）」の古墳名は、墳丘が方丘であることによる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。現在は[[宮内庁]]治定の皇族墓として同庁の管理下にある。 墳形は元々は方形であるが、後世に北東部に前方部が付加されて前方後円形に生垣が巡らされている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。一辺約85メートル{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}（または約90メートル[http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）]（橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」）。）・高さ約15メートルを測るが、これは方墳としては全国で最大規模になる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘は3段築成{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘表面では[[埴輪]]片が検出されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。その他については詳らかでない。 この桝山古墳は、[[古墳時代]]中期の[[5世紀]]前半頃（または5世紀末-[[6世紀]]前半頃{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}）の築造と推定される。被葬者は明らかでないが、前述のように現在は宮内庁により[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。本古墳の北方には[[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]]（[[宣化天皇]] 身狭桃花鳥坂上陵）が、西方には[[新沢千塚古墳群]]があり、それらとの関係性が指摘される{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。 == 被葬者 == 桝山古墳の実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]では第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の'''[[倭彦命]]'''（やまとひこのみこと）の墓に治定している宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}『陵墓地形図集成 縮小版』 宮内庁書陵部陵墓課編、学生社、2014年、p. 400。。倭彦命について、『日本書紀』では垂仁天皇28年に薨去して「身狭桃花鳥坂（むさのつきさか）」に葬られたとし、その際の殉死の悲惨な様によって殉死の禁令が出されたとする（埴輪起源説話の発端）{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠く{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。 その後、倭彦命墓の所在に関する所伝は失われ、[[江戸時代]]には『大和志』・『大和名所図会』等において[[鬼の爼・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）4月に[[内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、[[1886年]]（明治19年）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、[[1890年]]（明治23年）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。ただしその治定には否定的な見解が強い{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。 この桝山古墳は、[[古墳時代]]中期の[[5世紀]]前半頃（または5世紀末-[[6世紀]]前半頃{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}）の築造と推定される。被葬者は明らかでないが、前述のように現在は宮内庁により[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。本古墳の北方には[[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]]（[[宣化天皇]] 身狭桃花鳥坂上陵）が、西方には[[新沢千塚古墳群]]があり、それらとの関係性が指摘される{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。 == 被葬者 == 桝山古墳の実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]では第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の'''[[倭彦命]]'''（やまとひこのみこと）の墓に治定している宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}『陵墓地形図集成 縮小版』 宮内庁書陵部陵墓課編、学生社、2014年、p. 400。。倭彦命について、『日本書紀』では垂仁天皇28年に薨去して「身狭桃花鳥坂（むさのつきさか）」に葬られたとし、その際の殉死の悲惨な様によって殉死の禁令が出されたとする（埴輪起源説話の発端）{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠く{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。 その後、倭彦命墓の所在に関する所伝は失われ、[[江戸時代]]には『大和志』・『大和名所図会』等において[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）4月に[[内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、[[1886年]]（明治19年）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、[[1890年]]（明治23年）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。ただしその治定には否定的な見解が強い{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=石井茂輔|year=|chapter=身狭桃花鳥坂墓|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 30 奈良県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=[[大塚初重]]|year=1989|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[倭彦命]] * [[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）] - 橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」 {{デフォルトソート:ますやまこふん}} [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:橿原市の歴史]] [[Category:方墳]] [[Category:日本の陵墓]]

{{日本の古墳 |名称 = 桝山古墳 |画像 = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
左に墳丘、右に付加前方部 |別名 = |所属 = |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 28|緯度秒 = 29.47 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 46|経度秒 = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |形状 = [[方墳]] |規模 = 一辺85m
高さ15m |築造年代 = [[5世紀]]前半 |埋葬施設 = 不明 |被葬者 = （[[宮内庁]]治定）[[倭彦命]] |出土品 = [[埴輪]]片 |陵墓 = 宮内庁治定「身狭桃花鳥坂墓」 |特記事項 = 方墳としては全国第1位の規模 |地図 = Japan Nara |ラベル = 桝山古墳 |アイコン = 方墳 |ラベル位置 = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[倭彦命]]身狭桃花鳥坂墓 拝所}}]] '''桝山古墳'''（ますやまこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある古墳。形状は[[方墳]]。 実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]により「'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（むさのつきさかのはか、身狹桃花鳥坂墓）」として第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている。 方墳としては全国で最大規模の古墳である。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、貝吹山北麓の尾根先端部に築造された古墳である{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。「桝山（ますやま）」の古墳名は、墳丘が方丘であることによる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。現在は[[宮内庁]]治定の皇族墓として同庁の管理下にある。 墳形は元々は方形であるが、後世に北東部に前方部が付加されて前方後円形に生垣が巡らされている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。一辺約85メートル{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}（または約90メートル[http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）]（橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」）。）・高さ約15メートルを測るが、これは方墳としては全国で最大規模になる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘は3段築成{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。墳丘表面では[[埴輪]]片が検出されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}。その他については詳らかでない。 この桝山古墳は、[[古墳時代]]中期の[[5世紀]]前半頃（または5世紀末-[[6世紀]]前半頃{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989年}}）の築造と推定される。被葬者は明らかでないが、前述のように現在は宮内庁により[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981年}}。本古墳の北方には[[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]]（[[宣化天皇]] haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[倭彦命]]身狭桃花鳥坂墓 拝所}}]] '''桝山古墳'''（ますやまこふん）は、[[奈良県]][[橿原市]]鳥屋町にある古墳。形状は[[方墳]]。 実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]により「'''身狭桃花鳥坂墓'''（むさのつきさかのはか、身狹桃花鳥坂墓）」として第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている。 方墳としては全国で最大規模の古墳である。 == 概要 == [[奈良盆地]]南縁、貝吹山北麓の尾根先端部に築造された古墳である{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}。「桝山（ますやま）」の古墳名は、墳丘が方丘であることによる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}。現在は[[宮内庁]]治定の皇族墓として同庁の管理下にある。 墳形は元々は方形であるが、後世に北東部に前方部が付加されて前方後円形に生垣が巡らされている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}。一辺約85メートル{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}（または約90メートル[http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html 桝山古墳（崇神天皇皇子倭彦命墓）]（橿原市「かしはら探訪ナビ」）。）・高さ約15メートルを測るが、これは方墳としては全国で最大規模になる{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}。墳丘は3段築成{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}。墳丘表面では[[埴輪]]片が検出されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}。その他については詳らかでない。 この桝山古墳は、[[古墳時代]]中期の[[5世紀]]前半頃（または5世紀末-[[6世紀]]前半頃{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}）の築造と推定される。被葬者は明らかでないが、前述のように現在は宮内庁により[[倭彦命]]の墓に治定されている{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}。本古墳の北方には[[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]]（[[宣化天皇]] 身狭桃花鳥坂上陵）が、西方には[[新沢千塚古墳群]]があり、それらとの関係性が指摘される{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}。 == 被葬者 == 桝山古墳の実際の被葬者は明らかでないが、[[宮内庁]]では第10代[[崇神天皇]][[皇子]]の'''[[倭彦命]]'''（やまとひこのみこと）の墓に治定している宮内省諸陵寮編[{{NDLDC|1237123/8}} 『陵墓要覧』]（1934年、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション）8コマ。{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}『陵墓地形図集成 縮小版』 宮内庁書陵部陵墓課編、学生社、2014年、p. 400。。倭彦命について、『日本書紀』では垂仁天皇28年に薨去して「身狭桃花鳥坂（むさのつきさか）」に葬られたとし、その際の殉死の悲惨な様によって殉死の禁令が出されたとする（埴輪起源説話の発端）{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}。『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]では記載を欠く{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。 その後、倭彦命墓の所在に関する所伝は失われ、[[江戸時代]]には『大和志』・『大和名所図会』等において[[鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠]]東方の石室を倭彦命墓に比定する説もあった{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）4月に[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]によって現在の墓に定められ、[[1886年]]（明治19年）に宮内省（現・[[宮内庁]]）によって用地買収とともに同地にあった神社が移転され、[[1890年]]（明治23年）から修営された{{Sfn|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}。ただしその治定には否定的な見解が強い{{Sfn|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=石井茂輔|year=|chapter=身狭桃花鳥坂墓|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|身狭桃花鳥坂墓（国史）}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]] 30 奈良県の地名|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（平凡社）|1981}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=大塚初重|authorlink=大塚初重|year=1989|chapter=桝山古墳|title=[[日本古墳大辞典]]|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|桝山古墳（古墳）|1989}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[倭彦命]] * [[鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳]] == 外部リンク == * revision.text:
{{Ancient Kofun of Japan |Name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |Image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other Name = |Group = |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[ja:桝山古墳]]

{{Ancient Kofun of Japan |Name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |Image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other Name = |Group = |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:桝山古墳]]

Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other Name = |Group = |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[category:new pages]][[category:new pages]][[ja:桝山古墳]]

{{Ancient Infobox ancient site |Name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other Name = |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}} '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[ja:桝山古墳]]

15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[ja:桝山古墳]]

However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Square burial mounds]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[ja:桝山古墳]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - {{Infobox ancient site |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square burial mound|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square burial mound in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square burial mound |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient burial mound located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square burial mound in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Burial Mound Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square Kofun |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square Kofun |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = |Group = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |Latitude = 34|Latitude_min = 28|Latitude_sec = 29.47 |Longitude =135|Longitude_min = 46|Longitude_sec = 42.24 |ISO = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |Scale = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |Artifacts = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Imperial Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |Special Notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |Map = Japan Nara |Label = Masuyama Kofun |Icon = Square Kofun |Label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and designated as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular shape. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a designated imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square shape, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has designated the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, designated as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior designated the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] -
