15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |Burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual Interred_individual is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Interred_individual == Although the actual Interred_individual remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |Era = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kashihara]] [[Category:Square Kofun]] [[Category:Imperial mausoleums in Japan]] [[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 4]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 4]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:Masuyama Kofun]] [[Category:11]][[en:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[ja:桝山古墳]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:Completion tier 4]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 1]] [[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Masuyama Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Masuyama Kofun}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Masuyama Kofun}} [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Masuyama Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and other_designation1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a other_designation1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has other_designation1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, other_designation1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior other_designation1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this other_designation. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇|12=draft}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|12=draft}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=draft}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - {{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:02, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = 34|coordinates = 28|coordinates = 29.47 |coordinates =135|coordinates = 46|coordinates = 42.24 |omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=draft}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇|12=draft}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|12=draft}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=draft}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇|12=draft}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|12=draft}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=draft}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = Square Kofun |map_dot_label_Position = top }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara|ja|橿原市|12=draft}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁|12=draft}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|ja|埴輪|12=draft}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|12=draft}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|ja|宣化天皇|12=draft}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|ja|新沢千塚古墳群|12=draft}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=draft}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=draft}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=draft}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=draft}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] -
