This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], Toya-cho |coordinates = {{Coord|34.474853|N|135.7784|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], Toya-cho, [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]], or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]], and to the west is the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group]]. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]], or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]], and to the west is the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group]]. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]], or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]], and to the west is the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group]]. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], Toya-cho, [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]], or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]], and to the west is the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group]]. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - This was added through to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], Toya-cho |coordinates = {{Coord|34.474853|N|135.7784|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early [[:en:5th century|5th century]] |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in [[:en:Kashihara, Nara|Kashihara]], Toya-cho, [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early [[:en:5th century|5th century]], or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]]. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]], and to the west is the [[:en:Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group]]. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher=[[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Kashihara City Tourism {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|桝山古墳|version=93995770|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:桝山古墳]] {{Commons category|Masuyama Kofun}} {{wikidata link|Q48744177}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Square Kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - [[co:commons:Category:Masuyama Kofun]][[ja:桝山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Masuyama Kofun]][[ja:桝山古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Masuyama Kofun]][[ja:桝山古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name = Masuyama Kofun (桝山古墳) |image = [[File:Masuyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|280px]]
Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = {{Coord|34.474853|N|135.7784|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Nihon rekishi chimei taikei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - 15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353|1=Square Kofun}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|1=Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] - Mound on the left, added front section on the right |Other name = | part_of = |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}, Toya-cho |coordinates = {{Coord|34.474853|N|135.7784|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|omitted = JP-29 |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_size = 85m per side
15m in height |built = Early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} |burial Chamber = Unknown |Buried Person = (As determined by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} |burial goods = Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} |Mausoleum = Designated by the Imperial Household Agency as "Misano Tsukisaka no Haka" (身狭桃花鳥坂墓) |notes = Largest square Kofun in Japan |map = Japan Nara |map_dot_label = Masuyama Kofun |type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |map_dot_label_Position = top |architectural_styles = {{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353|1=Square Kofun}} }} [[File:Masuyama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}]] '''Masuyama Kofun''' (桝山古墳) is an ancient Kofun located in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}, Toya-cho, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The kofun is square-shaped and Historic Site1 as the "Imperial mausoleum of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}" by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. It is the largest square Kofun in Japan. == Overview == The Masuyama Kofun is situated on the northern slopes of Kaibuki Mountain, near the southern edge of the Nara Basin. The mound's name derives from its rectangular type. Currently managed by the Imperial Household Agency, the site is preserved as a Historic Site1 imperial burial site. Originally, the mound was a simple square type, but a front projection was later added to the northeast side, giving it a keyhole-like appearance. The mound measures approximately 85 meters per side and 15 meters in height, making it the largest square kofun in Japan. The mound is composed of three tiers. Fragments of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} have been found on its surface, but further details remain unclear. Archaeological studies date the kofun to the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|WD=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}}, or possibly between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. While the actual occupant is unknown, the Imperial Household Agency has Historic Site1 the site as the burial place of {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, a prince of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|WD=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}. To the north lies the {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}}, Historic Site1 as the tomb of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}, and to the west is the {{ill|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11502665|lt=Shinzawa Senchizuka Kofun Group|1=Niizawa Senzuka Kofun Cluster}}. Their spatial relationships suggest possible cultural or ritual connections. == Buried Person == Although the actual occupant remains unidentified, the Imperial Household Agency designates the site as the tomb of '''{{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}''', a son of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', he died in the 28th year of Emperor Suinin’s reign and was interred at Misano Tsukisaka, where his burial prompted an imperial prohibition on sacrificial burials due to their cruelty. The exact location of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto’s tomb became unclear over time. During the Edo period, theories emerged linking the tomb to nearby stone chambers such as {{ill|Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita and Oni-no-Secchin|lt=Oni-no-Manaita|ja|鬼の俎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-Manaita|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の俎・鬼の雪隠}}. In 1877, the Ministry of Interior Historic Site1 the current site as his mausoleum, and by 1886, the area was purchased and restored by the Imperial Household Agency. However, skepticism remains regarding the accuracy of this Historic Site. == References == {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Shigesuke Ishii|year=|chapter=Misano Tsukisaka no Haka|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kobunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kobunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Misano Tsukisaka no Haka}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1981|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|ja|日本歴史地名大系|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213509}} 30 Nara Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=4582490301|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Heibonsha)}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Hatsushige Otsuka|authorlink=Hatsushige Otsuka|year=1989|chapter=Masuyama Kofun|title={{ill|Nihon Kofun Daijiten|ja|日本古墳大辞典|1=Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kofun Daijiten|13=User:Immanuelle/Comprehensive Dictionary of Japanese Kofun|qq=}}|publisher={{ill|Tokyodo Shuppan|ja|東京堂出版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyodo Shuppan|1=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524645}}|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Masuyama Kofun (Kofun)}}}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko|lt=Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|ja|倭彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toriya Misanzai Kofun|lt=Toya Misanzai Kofun|ja|鳥屋ミサンザイ古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toya Misanzai Kofun|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_bunkazai/bunkazai/spot/masumaya.html Masuyama Kofun (Tomb of Yamatohiko-no-Mikoto)] -
